title: Geometry and Arithmetic - Estimates of periods of automorphic functions and subconvexity of L-functions
speaker: Joseph Bernstein

proof_stages.label: Motivation: subconvexity through automorphic periods
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates replacing direct study of L-functions by estimates for automorphic periods, with the aim of obtaining subconvexity bounds for small real groups such as PGL2(R).

proof_stages.label: Multiplicity-one setup and model functionals
proof_stages.summary: Gelfand and strong Gelfand pairs are defined so that H-equivariant morphism spaces are at most one-dimensional, allowing a standard model functional to be fixed up to a unit scalar in unitary representations.

proof_stages.label: Automorphic restriction and period coefficients
proof_stages.summary: For a lattice quotient X=Γ\G and a compact H-orbit, restriction of an automorphic realization of π to the orbit decomposes into H-automorphic representations, and each automorphic morphism is a scalar period coefficient times the model morphism.

proof_stages.label: Mean-value bound from Hermitian forms
proof_stages.summary: By comparing the Hermitian form obtained from restriction to the orbit with averaged translates bounded by the standard unitary form on E, one obtains general mean-value estimates for period coefficients, corresponding in L-function cases to convexity bounds.

proof_stages.label: Two spectral expansions from Gelfand triples
proof_stages.summary: For a chain of subgroups giving a Gelfand triple, a D-period functional has an expansion over the H-spectrum, and two different intermediate subgroups with the same G and D give two distinct spectral expansions of the same automorphic functional.

proof_stages.label: Positivity mechanism for extracting coefficient bounds
proof_stages.summary: Using a tensor-square construction π⊗π̄ to make coefficients positive, one chooses vectors whose model weights are peaked on a short interval while the dual weights are smooth, so the mean-value bound on one side controls short sums on the other.

proof_stages.label: Triple-product application and subconvexity estimate
proof_stages.summary: The method is carried out for a fourfold product of copies of G0 with two diagonal intermediate subgroups, yielding subconvex estimates for triple-product period coefficients and hence for the corresponding triple-product L-functions, after stationary-phase analysis of the model integrals.

proof_stages.label: Canonical-square-root interpretation of period factors
proof_stages.summary: The speaker observes that certain products of model functionals are independent of the intermediate model normalization, suggesting period coefficients whose squares are usual Euler-product L-values may themselves behave like canonical square-root or generalized L-functions.

examples.name: Torus period and Waldspurger-type L-value formula
examples.note: For G=PGL2 and H a torus attached to a quadratic extension, the squared period coefficient is described as an expression involving central L-values, illustrating why period estimates imply L-function estimates.
examples.engagement_reason: the torus period is cited with a Waldspurger-type L-value formula stated schematically, without deriving or checking it

passes.reason: The torus period/Waldspurger formula is presented as a standard instance connecting periods to central L-values.

examples.name: Rankin-Selberg formula as an unfolding special case
